agent_output:
No domain-specific ETA code exists in this codebase — this is a pure domain analysis request. I'll apply my logistics and courier domain expertise to produce the full analysis.

{"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to the delivery promise — the core contract between the courier service and the customer. In logistics, the ETA is not just a UI label; it is the service-level commitment that drives customer expectations, operational planning, and contractual obligations.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment — the context responsible for communicating delivery commitments to customers and coordinating last-mile operations.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival)","definition":"The projected date and/or time a package will be delivered to the recipient. Serves as the delivery promise."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"A time range within which delivery is expected. Can be expressed as a date range, a time-of-day range, or both."},{"term":"Delivery Promise","definition":"The commitment made to the customer about when their package will arrive. Forms the basis of SLA compliance measurement."},{"term":"SLA (Service-Level Agreement)","definition":"Contractual or published commitment defining delivery performance standards, often with penalties for breach."},{"term":"Last-Mile Delivery","definition":"The final leg of the delivery process from the local distribution hub to the recipient's address."},{"term":"On-Time Delivery Rate","definition":"The percentage of deliveries completed within the promised ETA. A key operational KPI."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Confirmation that a package was delivered, typically timestamped — used to measure SLA compliance against the ETA."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A delivery ETA must always be communicated to the customer after order confirmation. The format (timestamp vs. date-only) determines the granularity of the delivery promise.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without an ETA, the delivery has no measurable commitment. This is a system integrity requirement — every confirmed order must have a delivery promise.","testable_assertion":"Every order in status 'confirmed' or beyond must have a non-null ETA value associated with it."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"The on-time delivery metric must be measured against the same granularity as the ETA shown to the customer. If the ETA is date-only, on-time means 'delivered on or before that date (end of business or 23:59)'. If the ETA is a timestamp, on-time means 'delivered on or before that exact time'.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Misaligned measurement (e.g., measuring by timestamp when the customer was promised a date) corrupts operational reporting and SLA compliance data.","testable_assertion":"When ETA is date-only, a delivery completed at 23:59 on the promised date must be counted as on-time."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Changing ETA granularity retroactively on in-flight orders risks violating existing delivery promises. In-flight orders must retain the ETA format they were originally promised.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Changing a delivery promise mid-fulfillment without customer consent violates the commitment contract and could trigger SLA breaches.","testable_assertion":"Orders created before the format change must continue to display their original timestamp-based ETA until delivered."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Premium/express service tiers typically require time-specific ETAs (e.g., 'by 10:30 AM') as part of their value proposition. Removing timestamp precision from premium tiers degrades the service definition.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Service tier definitions are business policy — a VP could decide to redefine what premium means. But currently, time-specific delivery is the industry standard for premium courier services.","testable_assertion":"Orders with service tier 'express' or 'premium' must retain timestamp-granularity ETAs regardless of the default format change."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"B2B customers with contractual SLAs that reference time-of-day delivery windows cannot have their ETA granularity reduced without contract amendment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligations are legally binding. Unilateral changes to delivery promise format could constitute breach of contract.","testable_assertion":"Orders associated with B2B accounts that have active time-based SLA contracts must retain timestamp ETAs."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Any change to ETA display format must be consistently applied across all customer touchpoints: order confirmation email, tracking page, push notifications, and API responses.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Inconsistent ETA formats across channels creates customer confusion and potential disputes. The system is in a corrupt state if one channel says '14:30 Friday' and another says 'Friday'.","testable_assertion":"For a given order, all notification channels and tracking interfaces must display the same ETA granularity."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new standard-tier order is placed after the format change is deployed","when":"The customer views their delivery ETA on any channel","then":"The ETA is displayed as a date-only value (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday, March 27') with no time component"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An in-flight order was placed before the format change with a timestamp ETA","when":"The customer views their delivery ETA","then":"The original timestamp ETA is preserved and displayed (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday')"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A premium or express service tier order is placed","when":"The customer views their delivery ETA","then":"The ETA retains timestamp granularity regardless of the default format change"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A B2B customer with a time-based SLA contract places an order","when":"The ETA is generated and displayed","then":"The ETA includes time-of-day precision as required by the contract"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"An order's ETA is displayed as date-only","when":"The delivery is completed at any time on or before the promised date","then":"The delivery is recorded as on-time in operational metrics"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The ETA format change is deployed","when":"The same order's ETA is viewed across email, tracking page, push notification, and API","then":"All channels show the identical date-only format consistently"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A date-only ETA is shown to the customer","when":"The customer attempts to plan around the delivery (e.g., 'Will I get it before my 2 PM meeting?')","then":"The system does not promise or imply any time-of-day guarantee — only the date is committed"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed at 11:55 PM with next-day delivery — date-only ETA says 'tomorrow' but fulfillment window is only ~24 hours","expected_behavior":"The system must calculate the ETA date based on actual logistics feasibility, not just calendar math. If next-day is not achievable, the date must reflect the realistic delivery date."},{"scenario":"Customer in a different timezone than the fulfillment center — 'Friday' is ambiguous across timezones","expected_behavior":"The ETA date must be calculated and displayed in the recipient's local timezone. The system must store the timezone reference alongside the date."},{"scenario":"Delivery attempt fails on the promised date — what is the new ETA?","expected_behavior":"A new date-only ETA must be generated and communicated. The failed attempt should be recorded as a miss against the original promise for SLA purposes."},{"scenario":"API consumers (third-party integrators) currently parse timestamp ETAs — format change breaks their integration","expected_behavior":"API must maintain backward compatibility. Either version the API, or continue returning timestamp precision in API responses while showing date-only in customer-facing UIs."},{"scenario":"Same-day delivery orders — a date-only ETA of 'today' provides almost no useful information","expected_behavior":"Same-day delivery service tiers should retain time-window or timestamp ETAs. Date-only is insufficient for same-day promises."},{"scenario":"Multi-package orders where items ship separately — some arrive morning, some evening on the same date","expected_behavior":"Date-only ETA of 'Friday' is technically correct for all packages but may confuse customers expecting a single delivery. Clear per-package tracking must be maintained."},{"scenario":"Reporting and analytics dashboards currently aggregate by hour-of-day delivery patterns","expected_behavior":"Internal systems should continue to capture actual delivery timestamps at full precision. Only the customer-facing promise changes to date-only; operational data must remain granular."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Mixed impact. POSITIVE: Reduced SLA penalty exposure — a broader delivery window (full day vs. specific time) means fewer technical 'late' deliveries, potentially saving penalty costs. Lower operational cost from reduced need for precise time-slot scheduling. NEGATIVE: Potential revenue loss from premium customers who value time-specific delivery and may perceive service degradation. Risk of B2B contract renegotiation or disputes. Potential churn if competitors maintain time-specific ETAs as a differentiator.","operational":"POSITIVE: Significant reduction in operational pressure — dispatchers and drivers gain flexibility across the full day rather than racing specific time slots. Fewer 'failed on-time' incidents reduces exception handling overhead. Simplifies ETA calculation engine (fewer variables). NEGATIVE: Internal teams lose a forcing function for operational discipline. Without time-specific targets, route optimization and capacity planning may become less precise. On-time delivery KPI becomes less meaningful as a performance indicator when the window is an entire day.","user":"NEGATIVE for most customer segments. Customers overwhelmingly prefer more precise ETAs, not less. In the logistics industry, the trend is toward greater precision (Amazon, FedEx, UPS all moving to time windows or real-time tracking). This change moves against industry direction. Customers who need to be present for delivery (signatures, high-value items, perishables) are materially harmed — they cannot plan their day around a date-only ETA. PARTIAL POSITIVE: Fewer 'your delivery is late' notifications (since the window is wider), which reduces negative touchpoints.","risk":"HIGH RISK. (1) Competitive disadvantage — major carriers are increasing ETA precision, not decreasing it. (2) B2B contract breach risk for customers with time-based SLAs. (3) API breaking change risk for third-party integrators. (4) Regulatory risk in jurisdictions that mandate delivery time disclosure for certain goods (perishables, pharmaceuticals). (5) Reputational risk — perceived as a service downgrade. (6) Data integrity risk during migration — in-flight orders must be handled carefully to avoid ETA corruption.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers (B2C)","impact":"Reduced delivery predictability. Cannot plan around deliveries requiring presence. Perceived service downgrade, especially for time-sensitive deliveries."},{"group":"B2B / Enterprise Customers","impact":"Potential SLA contract violations if time-based commitments are removed. May trigger contract renegotiation, penalty claims, or churn."},{"group":"Customer Support Teams","impact":"Expected increase in 'when exactly will my delivery arrive?' inquiries. Support volume may spike as customers seek the precision the system no longer provides."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch Teams","impact":"Gain scheduling flexibility but lose precision targets. Risk of efficiency degradation without time-specific goals driving route optimization."},{"group":"Drivers / Couriers","impact":"Less time pressure per delivery, but potentially less clear prioritization during the day. May lead to end-of-day delivery clustering."},{"group":"Third-Party API Integrators","impact":"Breaking change if API response format changes. Requires versioning strategy and migration period."},{"group":"Product / Marketing Teams","impact":"Must reposition the change as a positive (reliability over precision) rather than a downgrade. Messaging is critical."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue Teams","impact":"Need to model impact on SLA penalties, premium tier revenue, and potential churn before committing to the change."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The proposal is underspecified. It states a desired change (timestamp to date-only) but does not clarify: (1) which service tiers are affected, (2) whether B2B contractual obligations have been reviewed, (3) what the migration strategy is for in-flight orders, (4) whether API consumers have been consulted, (5) what the business motivation is (cost reduction? accuracy improvement? complaint reduction?). Without understanding WHY this change is being proposed, I cannot confidently assess whether the trade-offs are acceptable. The change has significant cross-cutting business implications that require stakeholder alignment before implementation.","high_confidence_areas":["The invariant that in-flight orders must retain their original ETA format","The requirement that premium/express tiers need time-specific ETAs","The need for consistent ETA format across all channels","The risk to B2B customers with contractual SLAs"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the business has already consulted B2B customers and legal on contract implications","Whether same-day and express tiers are in scope or excluded","What the actual business motivation is — this affects whether the trade-offs are justified","Whether API backward compatibility has been considered","Regulatory requirements in specific operating jurisdictions"],"assumptions":["The system currently serves both B2C and B2B customers","Premium/express service tiers exist with time-specific delivery promises","There are third-party API consumers who parse ETA responses","The change is intended for customer-facing display, not internal operational data"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The proposal does not specify which service tiers are affected. Applying date-only ETAs to premium, express, or same-day tiers would fundamentally change their service definition. Are these tiers excluded from the change?","affected_stakeholders":["End Customers","B2B Customers","Product Teams","Revenue Teams"],"options":["A: Apply date-only to ALL tiers including premium/express","B: Apply date-only only to standard/economy tiers, preserve timestamp for premium/express/same-day","C: Make it configurable per service tier"],"recommendation":"Option B — preserve timestamp precision for premium and express tiers. Their value proposition depends on time-specific delivery promises."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No migration strategy specified for in-flight orders. What happens to orders already in the pipeline with timestamp-based ETAs?","affected_stakeholders":["End Customers","Customer Support","Engineering"],"options":["A: Retroactively convert all in-flight ETAs to date-only","B: Grandfather in-flight orders — only new orders get date-only ETAs","C: Give customers a choice during transition"],"recommendation":"Option B — grandfathering is the only approach that preserves existing delivery promises without risk of SLA violation."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The business motivation for this change is not stated. Is it to reduce SLA penalty exposure? Improve ETA accuracy (fewer 'wrong time' complaints)? Reduce operational complexity? The motivation fundamentally affects whether this trade-off is justified and how it should be implemented.","affected_stakeholders":["All stakeholders"],"options":["Provide the business case and data driving this proposal"],"recommendation":"Cannot fully evaluate trade-offs without understanding the problem this change is solving. Request the business case before proceeding."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information provided about B2B contractual obligations. If any B2B contracts specify time-of-day delivery windows, this change could constitute breach of contract.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Customers","Legal","Finance"],"options":["Review all active B2B SLA contracts for time-based delivery commitments before implementing"],"recommendation":"Legal and account management must review B2B contracts before this change can proceed for any B2B-eligible orders."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"API backward compatibility is not addressed. Third-party integrators may parse timestamp ETAs. Changing the format is a breaking change.","affected_stakeholders":["Third-Party API Integrators","Engineering","Partner Teams"],"options":["A: Change API response format (breaking change)","B: Keep API responses with timestamp precision, only change customer-facing UI","C: Version the API — new version returns date-only, old version preserved"],"recommendation":"Option C — API versioning with a deprecation timeline. Never break existing integrations without notice."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
